author: @agentbertram4769
text:
Thanks Robert, I agree with you completely.  Coming from the world of engineering one of my problems has been people who omit passing on some information by not adequately presenting their problem or their requirement.  I believe that they do this for two reasons.  The first is to hide their own ignorance (lack of knowledge).  The second is that they don't really know what they want.
Theory is essential, as if you know enough of it you are educated to a level where you are able  to solve the problem in hand.
However the practical approach of 'hitting something with a hammer' or 'applying a voltage across it' or 'poking it with a stick' is much more fun and keeps a person young at heart and happy.
Excellent video.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: spot on mate

Hello Robert
I found this video extremely helpful in my quest.

I'm solving a variety of problems related to my industry which I'm very passionate about.

What is the best way to introduce an invention to the market?

author: @arunjyotibanik7878
text: Thanks for your videos Robert. I very much liked your works on graphene but unlike you I am interested in Microbial Fuel Cells (you may have probably heard about it). In my Masters thesis I designed a new configuration of it with the most economic materials (those which are easily available in our local hardware stores) .......... to show the fact that with a 50 dollar investment I can increase power output about 25 times. But the power is in mW and I want it to increase it to kW range. Thats why I want to introduce graphene tech in it. Do you have any ideas, any ideas?? And btw does graphene exchange cations (free H+)???

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hiya Mate, I do know about MFCs and have some idea of their problems and the stage of current research. I would have to get up to speed a little bit but graphene tech is well researched as an approach to MFC improvement as scholar search will turn up loads of papers. Personally it think it is adsorbtion/deadsorbtion as opposed to true exchange

replies.author: @arunjyotibanik7878
replies.text: Thanks for your quick feedback. I have an idea of coating your conductive graphene ink over steel mesh, for not only capturing the electrons but also to conduct them better to the external circuit. Can I get a boost by implementing this??? I have also drawn a circuit, featuring an external power source connected to multiple anodes (oriented in a parallel circuit). Graphene does have excellent electrical conductivity, so  my hypothesis may work, don't u think?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Arunjyoti Banik
You are asking odd questions for someone who has done a masters on this - ask yourself the question - why are you using carbon? what functions does it perform?

replies.author: @arunjyotibanik7878
replies.text: Carbon or mainly graphite as anode, acts chiefly as a substratum for the microbes to adhere. And since it is conductive, it'll transfer electron to the circuit. I don't know the exact conductivity of Graphene inside a liquid environment but I suspect it'll be much higher than graphite. You have expertise on Graphene so thought of asking you for a sugg.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Arunjyoti Banik
yes - so performance is largely a function of surface area (SA) - what you need is to look at increasing the SA - the ink won't do that it has a 5% graphene content which is tiny and contains a lot of polymeric binders - I would think about treating acid carbon fibres which would perform both the jobs you want to perform - or alternatively look at the graphitic sugar foams which would also be good - well in the way I think of this problem it would be good - hope this helps

author: @VenkateshksDr
text:
Being an avid watcher of all your videos, and believe me it has been a great help i thought the following quote might be appropriate to you and to all the inventors and would be inventors: "A human being should be able to change a diaper, plan an invasion, butcher a hog, conn a ship, design a building, write a sonnet, balance accounts, build a wall, set a bone, comfort the dying, take orders, give orders, cooperate, act alone, solve equations, analyze a new problem, pitch manure, program a computer, cook a tasty meal, fight efficiently, die gallantly.Specialization is for insects."
-Robert A. Heinlein

author: @DrCureAging
text:
I totally agree. I find that theory sets a framework, but doesn't really put into context how it works in real life/applications. Like, people state a theory such as "telomere shortening causes aging"...however in real life, telomere shortening behaves in very complicated ways (which may or may not be true) and there are so many theories that contradict themselves. It gives no indication as how to physically deal with/engineer solutions. 

However, I also see Problem->Solution, and solution->problem to be like active/passive voice in english grammar. Learning to use both would greatly enhance a person's creativity since it would cover all aspects of both theory and practice.

author: @ochuspokus
text: This is the approach I prefer too, it is the engineers approach. This is why I studied engineering in university (electronics), but I dropped out because I couldn't stand how theoretical it is, and how much they focus on the exams. Everything you do is to prove that you can use maxwells equations or write matlab code or program in C, which I can, and I love doing that sort of thing ~ but only when it is orientated toward a project that I can choose myself. It's too much about analysing you in uni these days, it's too orwellian. In mathematics classes, they show you the theory, and then give you the problems. What a stupid idea! It should be showing you the problem, then showing examples, and then showing you the theory that you've proven by practice - The same way that the discoverer probably did it. No wonder kids in the UK can't stand mathematics classes. I have friends from working class backgrounds who play the game, Portal. Portal in case you don't know, is a puzzle game about solving mathematical/physical problems, but without using symbolic manipulation! They love it. You see, it's by presentation that we get people interested in interesting things.

author: @keeganmaloney3440
text:
Im thinking about the potential of making a nanostructure that is 'sympathetic' to a magnetic field. I don't know all the terminology in the world but the best I can explain it, it would align the dipoles similar to magnetic polarization, forming an energetically favorable bond,  except it would also form bonds that were not at 180% so the poles would not form perfect dipole regions. this could cause complex patterns to form in the intermolecular forces depending on the ratios of the compounds and the properties of your EM field. (would it be an AQ solution? maybe a ferrofluid?)

We could then examine the structures and figure out how to consistently make the ones we find the most useful. we might also be able to change there states temporarily by passing an em field through it and use it like a synthetic enzyme to process some compound. (Can't even static structures act as catalysts?)

replies.author: @keeganmaloney3440
replies.text: Isn't there an article somewhere about using an em field to induce something into a more structured state causing refrigeration? that would allow us to 'hold' the nanostructure in place. That could be very useful.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: aninteresting idea - are you looking for macro or micro control over the structure? - or even nano control

replies.author: @keeganmaloney3440
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith well probably nano control. The idea is to probably use it as a selective catalyst (like an enzyme) to make potentially anything. nutrition for humans and livestock, biopolymers, computational components, etc...

author: @MYNICEEV
text: Hello my friend. Not forgotten Elixir of Life. Will be sending soon. I must be unique (aimed at James). I get an idea. Build it. When it doesn't work. I find out why and do my level best to make it work. What keeps me going? Everyone who tells me "IT CAN'T BE DONE! IT WON'T WORK!  I want to make and learn from my own mistakes not others. Others mistakes can't be truly called mine when it doesn't work when I try to modify their mistake. I cite the following The Wright Brothers. Lots of flight theory to study. NOT! Alexander Graham Bell. Lots of telephone theory about telephony to study. NOT! All the others from the past that rolled up their sleeves and dug in. With no or very little theory to go on. If that was OK for them. Then it is OK for me and anyone else. Robert you just keep doing what your doing. We are out here amongst the nay sayers waiting for your next tutorial to learn from. Many many thanks my friend.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @MYNICEEV
replies.text: You are very welcome my friend.

author: @statebased
text:
Robert,
You are right to say dreaming is mostly a waste of time, but it is perfectly ok to get excited about an idea, and then have a bit of a disappointment when the idea doesn't work! In fact, I would argue that being emotional with your work is what will keep you going (for good or for bad!).
Also, you need enough theory to be efficient in your day to day work, and to keep yourself from trying to achieve the truly impossible. Of course, the fine line of impossible versus not impossible is the difference between crazy and brilliant, so that is a tricky one to master!
Thanks for sharing,
James

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Dude - are you deliberately being obtuse - I make these points and I would say it is obvious that I have a great deal of passion for what I do - as do many others and I do not denigrate this. What I am against is the person who dreams and never does  - the person who does and dreams is the one I admire

replies.author: @statebased
replies.text: No, no, sorry if it came over that way. I was just thinking of the "fresh out of school" people for which this is "not obvious". I have worked enough with teams implementing agile processes to know that many really take recommendation "very literally"! In fact, the main reason I really like your work is that it is "real stuff" and you do not hold back being excited about it!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sorry mate - i didn't mean to be an arse - i misread you - thanks for helping
